title: Deterministic retrieval recovers biomedical associations lost by language models
abstract:
ABSTRACT

Large language model (LLM)-based retrieval systems miss biomedical associations through output truncation, synonym mismatch and run-to-run variability, but the magnitude of this loss remains unclear. We present BioChirp, an open-source framework that uses LLMs for query interpretation and candidate filtering, combining multi-source consensus entity resolution with deterministic graph-based retrieval. Across four major biomedical databases, BioChirp recovered more associations with higher reproducibility than conventional LLM-based retrieval approaches.
ai_for_science_reason: The paper uses AI methods for improving retrieval in biomedical databases, contributing to scientific discovery in the biomedical domain.
implementation_justification: The framework is open-source and demonstrates improved retrieval performance.
review_justification: The paper provides a clear comparison with conventional methods.
benchmark_justification: The framework is tested across four major biomedical databases.
hypothesis_generation_justification: The framework aids in identifying associations that could lead to hypothesis generation.
scientific_discovery_justification: The method enhances retrieval of biomedical associations, aiding scientific discovery.
experiment_design_justification: The focus is on retrieval rather than direct experiment design.
material_discovery_justification: The paper does not address material discovery.
molecular_design_justification: The paper does not focus on molecular design.
drug_discovery_justification: The paper does not directly address drug discovery.
causal_discovery_justification: The retrieval of associations may indirectly support causal discovery.
biomedical_discovery_justification: The framework significantly improves retrieval of biomedical associations.
idea_development_justification: The improved retrieval can foster new ideas in biomedical research.
paper_title: Deterministic retrieval recovers biomedical associations lost by language models

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to present BioChirp, an open-source framework that uses language models for query interpretation and deterministic graph-based retrieval to recover biomedical associations that are often missed by conventional language model-based retrieval systems.
objective.evidence: We present BioChirp, an open-source framework that uses LLMs for query interpretation and candidate filtering, combining multi-source consensus entity resolution with deterministic graph-based retrieval.

knowledge_gap.answer: The knowledge gap addressed by the paper is the unquantified extent of biomedical associations missed by large language model-based retrieval systems due to output truncation, synonym mismatch, and run-to-run variability.
knowledge_gap.evidence: The question matters because missing associations can compromise downstream analyses in drug discovery and translational research, yet the extent of this loss has not been systematically quantified.

answer: BioChirp uses a deterministic graph-based retrieval approach instead of relying on language models for retrieval execution.
The framework incorporates a multi-source consensus entity-resolution module to improve accuracy in mapping entity mentions to canonical database identifiers.
BioChirp separates language model-based query interpretation from deterministic retrieval, ensuring reproducibility and completeness of results.
evidence: After candidate filtering and entity resolution, no language model participates in retrieval planning or execution.

A central technical component is a multi-source consensus entity-resolution module that maps entity mentions to canonical database identifiers through fuzzy lexical matching, semantic vector retrieval and curated synonym expansion.
Because the planner is deterministic, identical queries always produce identical structured outputs.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- Buniello et al. (2025) Open targets platform: facilitating therapeutic hypotheses building in drug discovery. (Methodological precursors)
- Davis et al. (2025) Comparative toxicogenomics database’s 20th anniversary: update 2025. (Experimental baselines)
- Liu et al. (2025) Hcdt 2.0: A highly confident drug-target database for experimentally validated genes, rnas, and pathways. (Experimental baselines)
- Zhou et al. (2024) Ttd: Therapeutic target database describing target druggability information. (Experimental baselines)
inspirational_papers.evidence: BioChirp currently supports four major biomedical databases: Open Targets, Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD), Highly Confident Drug-Target Database (HCDT) and Therapeutic Target Database (TTD).

steps.step: Interpret user query using language models
steps.output: Inferred intent and structured entity mentions
steps.evidence: At runtime, each user query was first routed to the interpretation module, which returned inferred intent and structured entity mentions.

steps.step: Expand entities through synonym and resolution modules
steps.evidence: These entities were then expanded through synonym and resolution modules.
The planner generated a source-specific retrieval strategy.

steps.step: Apply retrieval strategy to data sources
steps.evidence: Each retrieval module applied this plan to the corresponding data source.

steps.step: Return result set as a downloadable CSV file
steps.evidence: The complete result set was returned as a downloadable CSV file.

tools.name: Open Targets GraphQL API
tools.description: Used for querying Open Targets at runtime
tools.evidence: Open Targets was queried at runtime through a live GraphQL API.

tools.name: RapidFuzz
tools.description: Used for fuzzy lexical matching in entity resolution
tools.evidence: For each constrained field, the user-provided term is compared against the field-specific vocabulary of the target database using the Python library RapidFuzz.

benchmark_datasets.name: MedQA
benchmark_datasets.data_description: A standardized set of medical reasoning questions
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used to evaluate the quality of the language-model layer in BioChirp
benchmark_datasets.evidence: We used the MedQA benchmark, a standardized set of medical reasoning questions, as a proxy for this capability.

evaluation_metrics.name: Recall
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the ability to retrieve all relevant instances
evaluation_metrics.evidence: BioChirp configurations combining fuzzy, semantic and curated matching achieved the highest recall.

evaluation_metrics.name: F1 Score
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the balance between precision and recall
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Performance was quantified using recall and F1 score, computed separately for diseases, drugs and genes.

methods.name: Knowledge Extraction and Structurization
methods.description: Extracts and structures knowledge from user queries and databases.
methods.evidence: BioChirp uses a two-stage reasoning workflow to convert free-text questions into inputs suitable for deterministic retrieval.

methods.name: Experimental design generation
methods.description: Generates retrieval strategies and execution plans.
methods.evidence: The planner generated a source-specific retrieval strategy.

areas.name: Health Sciences
areas.description: The paper focuses on retrieving biomedical associations relevant to drug discovery and translational research.
areas.evidence: The question matters because missing associations can compromise downstream analyses in drug discovery and translational research.

areas.name: Applied Sciences & Engineering
areas.description: The paper develops a deterministic retrieval framework for biomedical databases.
areas.evidence: We present BioChirp, an open-source framework that uses LLMs for query interpretation and candidate filtering, combining multi-source consensus entity resolution with deterministic graph-based retrieval.

performance_summary.summary: BioChirp retrieved complete results for all six queries, whereas MCP-based systems frequently failed.
performance_summary.evidence: No MCP-based system returned a complete result set for any exhaustive query, whereas BioChirp retrieved complete results for all six queries.

baselines.name: MCP-based systems
baselines.description: Used as a baseline for retrieval completeness comparison.
baselines.evidence: We next benchmarked retrieval completeness against MCP-based systems using the official Open Targets MCP server.

benchmark_datasets.name: MedQA
benchmark_datasets.data_description: A standardized set of medical reasoning questions
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used to evaluate the quality of the language-model layer in BioChirp
benchmark_datasets.evidence: We used the MedQA benchmark, a standardized set of medical reasoning questions, as a proxy for this capability.

evaluation_metrics.name: Recall
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the ability to retrieve all relevant instances
evaluation_metrics.evidence: BioChirp configurations combining fuzzy, semantic and curated matching achieved the highest recall.

evaluation_metrics.name: F1 Score
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures the balance between precision and recall
evaluation_metrics.evidence: Performance was quantified using recall and F1 score, computed separately for diseases, drugs and genes.

benchmark_dataset.description: No traditional benchmark dataset was used.
benchmark_dataset.usage: The study used author-collected datasets and domain-specific resources.
benchmark_dataset.evidence: BioChirp currently supports four major biomedical databases: Open Targets, Comparative Toxicogenomics Database (CTD), Highly Confident Drug-Target Database (HCDT) and Therapeutic Target Database (TTD).

limitations.name: Limited Database Coverage
limitations.description: BioChirp's completeness is bounded by the coverage of its underlying databases.
limitations.evidence: BioChirp’s completeness is bounded by the coverage of its underlying databases, and its deterministic results apply to a fixed database state that may change as upstream resources are updated.

limitations.name: Ambiguous Names and Abbreviations
limitations.description: Ambiguous names and abbreviations can still challenge entity resolution.
limitations.evidence: Ambiguous names and abbreviations can still challenge entity resolution when multiple valid identifiers are plausible.

future_directions.name: Extend Framework to Other Databases
future_directions.description: Extend the framework to another structured biomedical database by defining schema, identifier mappings, and adapted prompts.
future_directions.evidence: Because language interpretation is separated from schema-defined retrieval, extending the framework to another structured biomedical database requires a schema definition, identifier mappings and adapted prompts.

resource_link.evidence: BioChirp source code is available at GitHub (github.com/abhi1238/biochirp).
